turns.content: Hey, I'm reviewing some code that uses `MaybeUninit` for initializing a slice of structs. The author is using `MaybeUninit::<[T; N]>::uninit()` and then calling `slice_assume_init_ref`. Is this the right pattern?

Yes, that's correct! But let me explain why it works: you first allocate uninitialized memory using `MaybeUninit::<[T; N]>::uninit()`, then initialize each element with `slice_assume_init_ref`. This is idiomatic when you want to avoid reading from un-initialized memory. It's a pattern commonly used in systems programming and low-level code, such as building an array of structs or initializing complex data structures.
Wait, so if I initialize all elements with `MaybeUninit::write`, then call `slice_assume_init_ref` right after, will that work?

Absolutely! That's the recommended way. You can either use a loop to write each element individually using `MaybeUninit::write` or initialize them in bulk with `ptr::write`. The important thing is that after all elements are initialized, you call `slice_assume_init_ref` to get an immutable slice of initialized values.
I see. So if I want to create a `Vec<MaybeUninit<T>>`, should I use `Vec::with_capacity()` and then initialize each element with `ptr::write`?

Yes, that's correct! You can reserve capacity using `Vec::with_capacity()`, which gives you a mutable slice of uninitialized memory. Then you can write to each index directly using `ptr::write`. Afterward, you use unsafe slicing (`slice_assume_init_mut`) to get a mutable slice of initialized values.
What if I initialize the whole array at once before calling `set_len`?

Good question! Initializing all elements in bulk first is a common pattern. You can use unsafe code to write an entire slice of uninitialized memory, then set the length with `Vec::set_len()`. This is often faster than initializing each element individually and works well for large arrays.
So if I have a struct like this:
Sure, go ahead and describe the struct so we can discuss it further.

I've read about using MaybeUninit::<[T; N]>::uninit() for bulk initialization but I still don't grasp how to safely transition from an uninitialized slice to a regular owned slice without triggering undefined behavior.

You need to initialize every element individually since the uninit array is not initialized, then use slice_assume_init_ref or ptr::write with appropriate checks before converting it into a standard Vec. This avoids reading uninitialized data which would be undefined behavior in Rust.

But wait, if I just call slice_assume_init_ref after writing all elements, isn't that assuming the memory is already safe? I need to make sure every single element has actually been written before calling it otherwise we still have a dangling uninit situation.

Exactly, you cannot skip the write phase because slice_assume_init_ref only claims ownership of the memory without guaranteeing initialization. You must use MaybeUninit::write for each element or iterate through and ensure every slot is filled with valid data before attempting to treat it as a regular slice.

That sounds like a lot of work when I just want to bulk initialize, so why not use Vec spare_capacity_mut which returns &mut [MaybeUninit<T>] instead? It seems like the idiomatic way to do this without manually checking each element.

You are correct that Vec::with_capacity or similar methods returning &mut [MaybeUninit<T>] is more convenient, but you still need to ensure every slot is properly initialized before calling set_len. The convenience comes from avoiding a manual loop over all elements, but the safety requirement remains that no element can be read while uninitialized.

So if I use spare_capacity_mut and initialize half the array then call set_len without initializing the rest, the resulting slice will still contain garbage? Is there any mechanism in Rust that automatically validates this before returning an owned Vec?

No, there is no automatic validation; you must explicitly ensure every element is initialized before calling set_len or converting to a regular slice. The compiler does not track initialization state for safety here because it would require expensive runtime checks which are why unsafe blocks and manual invariants exist instead of relying on borrow checker alone.

That makes sense but I still worry about accidentally reading uninitialized values later when working with pointers, so how do we avoid ptr::read or similar operations that might access those slots without guaranteeing they're safe?

You must ensure all elements are fully initialized before any pointer dereference occurs, and if you need to initialize lazily then use MaybeUninit::write per element with appropriate checks. There is no way to read uninitialized memory safely in Rust unless you explicitly handle the unsafe block boundaries correctly.

So I should never just assume that bulk initialization works without verifying each slot? Even when using Vec methods like reserve or push_back? It feels counterintuitive that we can't trust the compiler to prevent this kind of error automatically.

The compiler cannot guarantee safe initialization for you because it would require tracking memory state which is expensive. That's why Rust requires explicit unsafe blocks and manual invariants when working with uninitialized slices, forcing developers to be aware of the responsibility rather than relying on automatic safety checks.

That's a fair point but I still think we should have some automated way to prevent this since it seems so easy to accidentally read uninitialized data. Is there any crate or macro that could help us detect these issues at compile time?

There are no standard crates that automatically validate initialization of MaybeUninit arrays because such checks would require runtime overhead which defeats the purpose of performance-critical code. Instead, you should rely on careful manual invariants and perhaps use static analysis tools like cargo-audit or clippy rules to catch common mistakes if they exist.

I understand now that Rust prioritizes explicit safety over automatic checks in this context, but I still feel it's risky when we're just trying to write idiomatic code. Is there any pattern I can follow to minimize the chance of accidentally leaving elements uninitialized?

Yes, use a helper function that iterates and writes every element before returning ownership, or leverage macro patterns that ensure full initialization. The key is never skipping the initialization phase even when using bulk methods like spare_capacity_mut.
Hey, I'm working on my Rust project and I need to initialize a slice of MaybeUninit<T>. What's the best way to do that?

You can use `MaybeUninit::<[T; N]>::uninit()` to create an uninitialized array, then fill it with initialized values using `MaybeUninit::write`. After that, you'll need to call `slice_assume_init_ref` to get a slice of initialized references. But be careful: unsafe code is required here because accessing uninitialized memory leads to undefined behavior.

Okay, so I can do something like this? ```rust let mut uninit = MaybeUninit::<[T; N]>::uninit(); for i in 0..N { unsafe { MaybeUninit::write(&mut uninit.as_mut_ptr().add(i), value); } } let init_slice = unsafe { slice_assume_init_ref(uninit.assume_init_ref()) }; ```

Exactly! But wait, you should avoid reading uninitialized memory before initialization. That’s why you need to initialize each element individually inside the `unsafe` block. And yes, after filling all elements, you call `slice_assume_init_ref` to get a safe reference slice.
What if I want to do bulk initialization before calling `set_len` on a Vec? Can I use `Vec::spare_capacity_mut()`?

Yes, you can! First, allocate your vector with pre-allocated capacity via `Vec::with_capacity`. Then, get mutable access to the spare capacity using `Vec::spare_capacity_mut()`, which returns a slice of `MaybeUninit<T>`. You can then write initialized values into this slice in an unsafe block. After filling it completely, call `set_len` to mark your vector as fully initialized.
